reason: Both answers correctly diagnose the Goodhart's-law dynamic (bought signups not users, flat activated-user count), recommend paying on activation, segmenting referred vs. organic, and tracking cost-per-activated-user instead of cost-per-signup — hitting all the key points roughly equally well. The difference is in the details of the 'split the bonus' fix: Answer A proposes $5 at signup / $15 at activation, which reintroduces a signup-time incentive that partially undermines the very fix it's proposing (still rewards low-intent bounty hunters for merely signing up). Answer B's split (rewarding referrer only on activation, plus giving the referee skin in the game) stays internally consistent with 'pay on activation.' B also more concretely addresses referrer-side gaming with capping/tiering payouts per referrer and fraud signals (device/payment fingerprints), which better covers the 'segment/police who refers' consideration. Both are otherwise comparable in concision and clarity.
